content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 15, 2010

Christine M. Nolan
CEO, Spot-On
1819 Polk Street, Suite 317
San Francisco, CA 94109

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-194

Dear Ms. Nolan:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ As CEO of Spot-On, you are requesting
advice on behalf of the company.

QUESTION

Must a company that purchases online display advertising on behalf of a candidate
provide the candidate with information regarding payments made to Internet publishers that host
the ads?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Payments to vendors that host the ads must be reported by the candidate as
expenditures made to subvendors. Under Regulation 18431(d), vendors that make such
expenditures are required to provide the campaign committee the information required for
reporting these expenditures.

FACTS

Spot-On places online display advertising for political campaigns and candidates. Spot-
On contracts with candidates to manage and control the distribution of these ads to "Internet

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-194
Page No. 2

publishers"2 and uses a leased ad server for this purpose. Spot-On pays Internet publishers to
publish the ads on their websites.

In our telephone conversation of December 2, 2010, you stated that Spot-On can be
characterized as the online version of a television media buyer. However, unlike buys made by
television buyers who select and buy time from a specific station at specific times or time
intervals, website buys are not made by individuals, but are, instead directed by a computer
server. The computer directs ads to websites in Spot-On's database that are likely to attract
targeted voters. If an Internet publisher's website fails to deliver the desired traffic, as measured
by various factors, including the number of times and ad sends a reader to the candidate's website,
the computer redirects the ad to other Internet publishers' websites. Spot-On, which negotiates
prices with Internet publishers, pays the Internet publishers based on results. The candidate pays
Spot-On a fee that covers payments to the Internet publishers as well as payment for Spot-On's
services. A candidate does not know who the publishers are or the amounts they are paid.

Candidates and committees have the information needed to report payments to Spot-On
as expenditures. Spot-On does not provide information to candidates regarding its payments to
Internet publishers. If Spot-On is required to provide this information, the pricing it has
negotiated would be available to competitors, who could then undercut Spot-On's pricing. Spot-
On considers this pricing information to be propriety information.

You state that the services Spot-On provides are no different from those of ad networks
or exchanges working on the Internet, such as Burst Media, Tribal Fusion, Google, Yahoo and
AOL. Like these networks or exchanges, Spot-On buys ads in bulk via computerized
marketplaces. You are not aware of any ad networks or exchanges that provide detailed lists of
their buys. You also state that these networks and exchanges consider this information to be
proprietary information.

ANALYSIS

Section 84211 sets forth the required content of campaign reports filed by
candidates and committees. Generally, committees are required to itemize campaign
expenditures of $100 or more on their campaign reports.

In addition to requiring committees to report direct expenditures they make, the Act also
requires committees to report expenditures made by an agent or independent contractor (such as
a campaign worker, advertising agency, or campaign management firm) on behalf of the
candidate or committee. (Sections 84211(k)(6) and 84303; regulation 18431.). For example, if a
campaign consultant purchases television time, the committee must report both the direct
payment to the consultant and the subvendor payment – the consultant's payment to the
television station. (Everman Advice Letter, No. 1-02-012.)

_________________________
2 "Internet publishers" sell space on their websites for online ads to appear.

File No. A-10-194
Page No. 3

Section 84303 states:

"No expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more shall be made, other than
overhead or normal operating expenses, by an agent or independent contractor,
including, but not limited to, an advertising agency, on behalf of or for the benefit
of any candidate or committee unless it is reported by the candidate or committee
as if the expenditure were made directly by the candidate or committee. The
agent or independent contractor shall make known to the candidate or committee
all information required to be reported by this section."

Section 84211(k)(6) requires committees to disclose the name, address, amount and a
description for expenditures to each person, if different from the payee, who has provided
consideration for an expenditure of $500 or more. This regulation applies to payments made by
Spot-On to Internet publishers. Regulation 18431(d) requires vendors to provide the information
needed by the candidate or committee in order to disclose the information on its campaign
statements.

You believe that Spot-On's purchase of website space for political ads should not be
treated the same as purchases of television time under Section 18431. You base this belief on the
facts that (i) Spot-On's buys are directed by computer and (ii) payment for the ads is made only
after the success of the placement is ascertained. However, we find no basis in the Act for
making a distinction. Buys directed by computer simply use a different method of selecting and
purchasing ad space. Payment that is made after the ad has run only affects the timing for
reporting these payments. These differences do not change the nature of ad placement. We
interpret the reporting requirements of subvendor information to apply to payments made by
Spot-On to Internet publishers in the same way that these requirements apply to payments made
to newspapers or television stations.

Accordingly, Spot-On's payments made to Internet publishers on behalf of a candidate
must be reported by the candidate as expenditures made to subvendors. Spot-On is, therefore,
required to provide the necessary information to the candidate.

With respect to your belief that ad networks and exchanges do not supply subvendor
information to candidates, we believe that they are required to do so if they are buying website
space on behalf of a candidate or committee.

Finally, we recognize your concerns about the public release of proprietary information
and that it is perhaps possible that some balance could be struck on this issue so the public is
provided meaningful information on campaign expenditures without the unnecessary disclosure
of this information. However, since the broad reporting requirements are in the statute, striking
this balance would require a legislative change.

File No. A-10-194
Page No. 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl
